------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Single-Cell RNA-seq Data – Complete Guide Part 1: From FASTQ to Count Matrix**

*Author: Ghad Ahmed*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Bulk vs. scRNA-seq

-   Bulk (TruSeq): high transcript coverage, no single-cell resolution
-   scRNA-seq: single-cell resolution, lower transcript capture, higher cost
-   Choice depends on trade-off between coverage, resolution & budget

### Applications of scRNA-seq

-   **Heterogeneity** → identifies distinct cell populations; maps cell types in health & disease (e.g. tumors, immune cells)
-   **Lineage Tracing** → tracks cell transitions over time; uses pseudotime analysis for differentiation trajectories
-   **Stochastic Gene Expression** → identical cells can behave differently due to random gene activity fluctuations

### scRNA-seq Methods Comparison

| Method       | Capture Efficiency | Cost/Cell | Best For                          |
|--------------|--------------------|-----------|-----------------------------------|
| inDrops      | 70–80%             | \$0.06    | Cost-effective, customizable      |
| 10x Genomics | 50–70%             | \$0.20    | Speed, high throughput            |
| Drop-Seq     | \~10%              | \$0.06    | Budget workflows                  |
| Seq-Well     | \~80%              | \$0.15    | High efficiency, non-droplet      |
| SMART-seq    | \~80%              | \$1.00    | Full-length transcripts, isoforms |

### Spatial Transcriptomics

-   scRNA-seq loses tissue location information
-   Spatial transcriptomics adds spatial coordinates to gene expression data
-   Reveals where genes are expressed within tissue → maps cell types, tissue organization & cell–cell interactions

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 1: Install Supporting Software

``` bash
conda create -n sc python=3.10
conda activate sc
conda config --add channels defaults
conda config --add channels bioconda
conda config --add channels conda-forge
conda config --set channel_priority strict
conda install -y sra-tools fastqc multiqc wget samtools

# Verify
fastqc --version    # FastQC v0.12.1
multiqc --version   # multiqc, version 1.19
prefetch --version  # prefetch : 3.0.10
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 2: Download Reference Genome

``` bash
mkdir -p ~/references/cellranger
cd ~/references/cellranger
wget https://cf.10xgenomics.com/supp/cell-exp/refdata-gex-GRCh38-2024-A.tar.gz
tar -xzvf refdata-gex-GRCh38-2024-A.tar.gz
# genome.fa → Reference genome FASTA
# genes.gtf → Gene annotations
# star/      → Pre-built STAR aligner index
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 3: Create Project Directory Structure

``` bash
mkdir -p ~/PBMC_scRNA && cd ~/PBMC_scRNA
mkdir -p raw_data fastqc_reports cellranger_output scripts logs star_index star_output counts
tree -L 1 .
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 4: Download 10x PBMC Dataset

``` bash
cd ~/PBMC_scRNA/raw_data
wget https://cf.10xgenomics.com/samples/cell-exp/7.0.1/SC3pv3_GEX_Human_PBMC/SC3pv3_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs.tar
tar -xf SC3pv3_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs.tar
cd Chromium_3p_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs && ls -lh
# I1_001.fastq.gz → Index read (usually not needed)
# I2_001.fastq.gz → Second index read
# R1_001.fastq.gz → Barcodes + UMI (MOST IMPORTANT)
# R2_001.fastq.gz → Biological reads (gene sequence)
```

> **Note:** If using SRA files, rename to 10x convention: `SRRxxxx_1.fastq.gz → SampleName_S1_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz` `SRRxxxx_2.fastq.gz → SampleName_S1_L001_R2_001.fastq.gz`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 5: Quality Control with FastQC

``` bash
cd ~/PBMC_scRNA
fastqc raw_data/Chromium_3p_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs/*_R2_001.fastq.gz \
    --outdir fastqc_reports --threads 8 --quiet

multiqc fastqc_reports \
    --outdir fastqc_reports \
    --filename multiqc_report \
    --title "SC3pv3_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs_Quality_Control"
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 6: Build chr22-Only Reference for STARsolo

> **Why chr22 only?** 16GB RAM is insufficient for full genome alignment. chr22 subset validates the pipeline with minimal resources.

``` bash
mkdir -p ~/references/chr22 && cd ~/references/chr22

awk '/^>chr22/{flag=1} /^>chr/{if($0!~"^>chr22") flag=0} flag' \
    ~/references/cellranger/refdata-gex-GRCh38-2024-A/fasta/genome.fa > chr22.fa

zgrep -w "chr22" \
    ~/references/cellranger/refdata-gex-GRCh38-2024-A/genes/genes.gtf.gz \
    > ~/references/chr22/chr22.gtf

ls -lh
# chr22.fa  ~50M
# chr22.gtf ~34M

gunzip -c ~/references/cellranger-10.0.0/lib/python/cellranger/barcodes/3M-february-2018_TRU.txt.gz \
    > ~/references/chr22/3M-february-2018.txt
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 7: Subset FASTQs (10M Reads for Practice)

> **Why subset?** Full dataset \~350M reads = 30+ hours on 16GB RAM. 10M reads ≈ 1 hour, sufficient for chr22 practice.

``` bash
cd ~/PBMC_scRNA

zcat raw_data/Chromium_3p_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs/Chromium_3p_GEX_Human_PBMC_S1_L001_R2_001.fastq.gz \
    | head -40000000 | gzip > raw_data/R2_subset.fastq.gz

zcat raw_data/Chromium_3p_GEX_Human_PBMC_fastqs/Chromium_3p_GEX_Human_PBMC_S1_L001_R1_001.fastq.gz \
    | head -40000000 | gzip > raw_data/R1_subset.fastq.gz
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 8: Build STARsolo chr22 Index

> `--genomeSAindexNbases 11` is tuned for chr22 size. Full genome uses default 14.

``` bash
STAR \
    --runMode genomeGenerate \
    --genomeDir ~/PBMC_scRNA/star_index \
    --genomeFastaFiles ~/references/chr22/chr22.fa \
    --sjdbGTFfile ~/references/chr22/chr22.gtf \
    --runThreadN 8 \
    --genomeSAindexNbases 11
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Step 9: Run STARsolo Alignment and Counting

> **Important:** R2 goes first, R1 goes second (opposite of intuition) R2 = cDNA biological read \| R1 = barcode + UMI

``` bash
STAR \
    --runMode alignReads \
    --genomeDir ~/PBMC_scRNA/star_index \
    --readFilesIn \
        ~/PBMC_scRNA/raw_data/R2_subset.fastq.gz \
        ~/PBMC_scRNA/raw_data/R1_subset.fastq.gz \
    --readFilesCommand zcat \
    --soloType CB_UMI_Simple \
    --soloCBwhitelist ~/references/chr22/3M-february-2018.txt \
    --soloCBstart 1 --soloCBlen 16 \
    --soloUMIstart 17 --soloUMIlen 12 \
    --outSAMtype BAM SortedByCoordinate \
    --outSAMattributes NH HI nM AS CR UR CB UB GX GN sS sQ sM \
    --outFileNamePrefix ~/PBMC_scRNA/star_output/ \
    --runThreadN 4 \
    --limitBAMsortRAM 8000000000
```

**Expected output:**

```         
star_output/Solo.out/Gene/
├── filtered/
│   ├── barcodes.tsv   ← valid cell barcodes
│   ├── features.tsv   ← gene names/IDs
│   └── matrix.mtx     ← count matrix
└── raw/
    ├── barcodes.tsv
    ├── features.tsv
    └── matrix.mtx
```

> Use `filtered/` for Seurat — empty droplets already removed.

------------------------------------------------------------------------
